name: Indecomposable Group
statement: A group G is indecomposable when G ≠ 1 and G = A ⊕ B implies A = 1 or B = 1.

name: Dedekind domain
statement: A Dedekind domain is an integral domain that is not a field, Noetherian, integrally closed in its field of fractions, and every nonzero prime ideal is maximal.
proof_sketch: This is a definition; no proof is required. Equivalent characterizations include: every nonzero proper ideal factors uniquely as a product of prime ideals.

name: Roth's Theorem
statement: If α is an algebraic number of degree ≥ 2, then for each fixed ε > 0 there are at most finitely many rational numbers p/q, q > 0, such that |α - p/q| < 1/q^{2+ε}. Consequently, there exists a constant c > 0 such that for all rationals p/q, |α - p/q| > c/q^{2+ε}.
proof_sketch: Not provided (deep result by K. F. Roth, 1955).

name: Syzygy Module
statement: For a set of polynomials f₁,...,f_t in a ring R, the syzygy module Syz(f₁,...,f_t) is the set of all (h₁,...,h_t) ∈ R^t such that h₁f₁ + ... + h_t f_t = 0.
proof_sketch: This is a definition; no proof is required.

name: Ramification Degree at a Point
statement: The ramification degree of a nonconstant morphism h : C → C' at a point P ∈ C is e_P(h) = ν_P(t' ∘ h), where t' is a uniformizer at h(P) and ν_P is the discrete valuation on the function field of C.
proof_sketch: Definition; no proof.

name: Description of Subgroup Generated by a Set
name_norm: description of subgroup generated by a set
statement: If G is a group and X is a nonempty subset of G, then the subgroup ⟨X⟩ generated by X consists of all finite products a_1^{n_1} a_2^{n_2} ... a_t^{n_t} (a_i ∈ X; n_i ∈ ℤ). In particular, for every a ∈ G, ⟨a⟩ = {a^n | n ∈ ℤ}.
proof_sketch: Let H be the set of all such finite products. Show H is a subgroup of G containing X and contained in every subgroup that contains X. Therefore H ≤ ⟨X⟩ ≤ H, yielding equality.

name: K-algebra (Algebra over K)
statement: Let K be a commutative ring with identity. A K-algebra (or algebra over K) A is a ring A such that: (i) (A,+) is a unitary (left) K-module; (ii) k(ab) = (ka)b = a(kb) for all k ∈ K and a,b ∈ A. A K-algebra A which, as a ring, is a division ring, is called a division algebra. An algebra over a field K that is finite dimensional as a vector space over K is called a finite dimensional algebra over K.
proof_sketch: None (definition).

name: Definition of a Homomorphism of Semigroups
name_norm: definition of a homomorphism of semigroups
statement: Let G and H be semigroups. A function f: G -> H that preserves the given algebraic structure (i.e., f(ab) = f(a)f(b) for all a,b in G) is called a homomorphism.
proof_sketch: None (definition)

name: Description of Principal Ideal
statement: Let R be a ring and a ∈ R. The principal ideal (a) consists of all finite sums of elements of the forms na (n ∈ Z), ra, as, and r a s (r,s ∈ R).
proof_sketch: Show the set S of all such finite sums satisfies the ideal criteria, contains a, and is contained in every ideal containing a; thus S = (a).

name: Purely transcendental extension
statement: A field F is called a purely transcendental extension of a field K if F = K(S), where S ⊂ F and S is algebraically independent over K.
proof_sketch: N/A

name: Equicardinality of finite transcendence bases
statement: Let F be an extension field of K. If S is a finite transcendence base of F over K, then every transcendence base of F over K has the same number of elements as S.
proof_sketch: Proof continues beyond the excerpt provided.

name: Middle Linear Map
statement: If A_R and _RB are modules over a ring R, and C is an (additive) abelian group, then a middle linear map from A × B to C is a function f : A × B → C such that for all a, a_i ∈ A, b, b_i ∈ B, and r ∈ R: f(a_1 + a_2, b) = f(a_1, b) + f(a_2, b); f(a, b_1 + b_2) = f(a, b_1) + f(a, b_2); f(ar, b) = f(a, rb).
proof_sketch: N/A

name: Tensor Product (Definition 5.1)
statement: Let A be a right module and B a left module over a ring R. Let F be the free abelian group on the set A × B. Let K be the subgroup of F generated by all elements of the following forms: [text incomplete in provided excerpt].
proof_sketch: N/A

name: Quotient Monoid/Group by a Congruence
statement: Let R be an equivalence relation on a monoid G such that a1 ~ a2 and b1 ~ b2 imply a1 b1 ~ a2 b2 for all a_i, b_i ∈ G. Then the set G/R of equivalence classes with operation (a)(b) = ab is a monoid. Moreover, if G is a group, G/R is a group; if G is abelian, G/R is abelian.
proof_sketch: First, well-definedness: if a1 = a2 and b1 = b2, then a1 ~ a2 and b1 ~ b2, so a1 b1 ~ a2 b2, giving a1 b1 = a2 b2. Associativity: a (b c) = a(bc) = a(bc) = (ab)c = (ab)c = (a b) c. Identity: e = e_G gives a e = ae = a and e a = ea = a. If G is a group, then a a^{-1} = a a^{-1} = e, so a^{-1} is the inverse. Abelian property is inherited directly.

name: Radical of a Primary Submodule is a Primary Ideal
name_norm: radical of a primary submodule is a primary ideal
statement: Let R be a commutative ring with identity and A a primary submodule of an R-module B. Then QA = { r ∈ R | rB ⊆ A } is a primary ideal in R.
proof_sketch: Since A ≠ B, 1R ∉ QA, so QA ≠ R. If rs ∈ QA and s ∉ QA, then sB ⊈ A, so there exists b ∈ B with sb ∉ A but r(sb) ∈ A. Since A is primary, rnB ⊆ A for some n; that is, rn ∈ QA. Therefore QA is primary.

name: Center of Nontrivial Finite p-Groups (Corollary 5.4)
statement: The center C(G) of a nontrivial finite p-group G contains more than one element.
proof_sketch: Class equation: |G| = |C(G)| + Σ [G:C_G(x_i)]. Each [G:C_G(x_i)] > 1 divides |G| = p^n, so p divides each and |G|, thus p | |C(G)|. Since |C(G)| ≥ 1, we have |C(G)| ≥ p.

name: Theorem 1.6 (Structure of Subgroups of Finitely Generated Free Abelian Groups)
name_norm: structure of subgroups of finitely generated free abelian groups
statement: If F is a free abelian group of finite rank n and G is a nonzero subgroup of F, then there exists a basis {x_1, ..., x_n} of F, an integer r (1 ≤ r ≤ n), and positive integers d_1, ..., d_r such that d_1 | d_2 | ... | d_r and G is free abelian with basis {d_1 x_1, ..., d_r x_r}.
proof_sketch: By induction on n. If n=1, G = ⟨d_1 x_1⟩. For n>1, let S be the set of integers s such that some basis {y_i} yields an element in G with coefficient s for y_1. Let d_1 be the least positive element in S. Using Lemma 1.5, choose basis {x_1, y_2, ..., y_n} with d_1 x_1 ∈ G, set H = ⟨y_2, ..., y_n⟩, so F = ⟨x_1⟩ ⊕ H. Prove G = ⟨d_1 x_1⟩ ⊕ (G ∩ H). Apply the induction hypothesis to G ∩ H in H, obtaining a basis {x_2, ..., x_n} of H and d_2 | ... | d_r with G ∩ H = ⟨d_2 x_2, ..., d_r x_r⟩. Minimality of d_1 forces d_1 | d_2. Then {x_1, ..., x_n} and {d_1 x_1, ..., d_r x_r} are the required bases.

name: Inner Automorphism and Center Theorem
statement: (i) For each g ∈ G, conjugation by g induces an automorphism of G. (ii) There is a homomorphism G → Aut G whose kernel is C(G) = {g ∈ G | gx = xg for all x ∈ G}.
proof_sketch: (i) Act on G by conjugation. The map T_g(x) = gxg⁻¹ is a bijection by Theorem 4.5 and a homomorphism since g(xy)g⁻¹ = (gxg⁻¹)(gyg⁻¹). Thus T_g ∈ Aut G. (ii) The image of the homomorphism G → A(G) lies in Aut G. Its kernel is {g | T_g = id} = {g | gxg⁻¹ = x ∀x} = C(G).

name: Definition of Contraction and Lies Over
name_norm: definition of contraction and lies over
statement: If S is an extension ring of R and I is an ideal of S, then I ∩ R is an ideal of R called the contraction of I to R, and I is said to lie over J = I ∩ R. If Q is a prime ideal in S, then Q ∩ R is a prime ideal of R.
proof_sketch: N/A

name: Characterization of Injective Modules over Rings with Identity (Baer's Criterion)
name_norm: characterization of injective modules over rings with identity (baers criterion
statement: Let R be a ring with identity. A unitary R-module J is injective if and only if for every left ideal L of R, any R-module homomorphism f: L -> J may be extended to an R-module homomorphism R -> J.
proof_sketch: If J is injective, the extension property follows directly. Conversely, assume extension property for left ideals. Given a monomorphism g: A -> B and f: A -> J, let S be the set of partial extensions h: C -> J with Im g ⊆ C ⊆ B. Partially order S by extension. S is nonempty since f∘g^{-1}: Im g -> J works. By Zorn's Lemma, a maximal element h: H -> J exists. If H ≠ B, pick b∈B\H, set L = { r∈R | rb∈H } a left ideal. Map r ↦ h(rb) extends to k: R->J by hypothesis. Let c = k(1_R). Define extension on H+Rb by a+rb ↦ h(a)+rc, contradicting maximality. Hence H=B.

name: Characterization of Invertible Matrices as Matrices of Isomorphisms
name_norm: characterization of invertible matrices as matrices of isomorphisms
statement: Let R be a ring with identity and E, F free left R-modules with ordered bases V, V′ respectively such that |V| = n = |V′|. For A ∈ Mat_n R, A is invertible if and only if A is the matrix of an isomorphism f: E → F relative to V and V′. In this case A^{-1} is the matrix of f^{-1} relative to V′ and V.
proof_sketch: A homomorphism f is an isomorphism iff it has a two-sided inverse f^{-1}. The matrix of the composition equals the product of matrices; using I_n as matrix of identity maps, A invertible corresponds to existence of inverse map.

name: Natural Anti-isomorphism to Opposite Ring
statement: The identity map on the underlying set, r ↦ r, is an anti-isomorphism from a ring R to its opposite ring R^op.
proof_sketch: The map is bijective and additive. For multiplication: (ab) ↦ ab in R, while a ∘ b = ba, so θ(ab) = ab = b ∘ a = θ(b) ∘ θ(a), satisfying the anti-homomorphism condition.

name: Greatest common divisor in a unique factorization domain
name_norm: greatest common divisor in a unique factorization domain
statement: Let a_1, …, a_n be nonzero elements of a unique factorization domain. Write each a_i = u_i p_1^{m_{1i}} p_2^{m_{2i}} ⋯ p_t^{m_{ti}} with a unit u_i and distinct prime elements p_j. Then c = p_1^{k_1} p_2^{k_2} ⋯ p_t^{k_t}, where k_j = min_i m_{ji}, is a greatest common divisor of a_1, …, a_n.
proof_sketch: The element c divides each a_i because the exponent of each prime in c is the minimum of the exponents in the a_i. Conversely, any common divisor must have each prime exponent at most the minimum, so it divides c.

name: Primary ideal definition
statement: An ideal P in a ring R is called primary if whenever ab ∈ P and a ∉ P, there exists a positive integer n such that b^n ∈ P.

name: Dedekind Domain Definition
statement: A Dedekind domain is an integral domain R in which every ideal (≠ R) is the product of a finite number of prime ideals.
proof_sketch: None

name: Intersection of Integrally Closed Subrings
statement: Let T be a commutative ring with identity and {S_i}, {R_i} families of subrings such that T is an extension ring of S_i and S_i is an extension ring of R_i for every i. If each R_i is integrally closed in S_i, then ∩ R_i is integrally closed in ∩ S_i.
proof_sketch: Take any element x in ∩ S_i integral over ∩ R_i. Its monic polynomial has coefficients in ∩ R_i ⊆ each R_i, so x is integral over each R_i. Since R_i is integrally closed in S_i, x ∈ R_i for all i, hence x ∈ ∩ R_i.

name: Correspondence Between Contravariant and Covariant Functors
name_norm: correspondence between contravariant and covariant functors
statement: Every contravariant functor S: C → D induces a unique covariant functor S̄: C^{op} → D defined by S̄(A)=S(A) and S̄(f^{op})=S(f). Conversely, every covariant functor on C^{op} arises uniquely from a contravariant functor on C.
proof_sketch: Verification of functor axioms using the definition of opposite category composition.

name: Functor of Several Variables (Mixed Variance)
statement: A functor T of two variables, contravariant in the first and covariant in the second, from C × D to E assigns an object T(C,D) and for each f: C→C', g: D→D' a morphism T(f,g): T(C',D) → T(C,D') satisfying T(1_C,1_D)=1_{T(C,D)} and T(f'∘f, g'∘g)=T(f,g')∘T(f',g).
proof_sketch: N/A - Definition

name: Maschke's Theorem
statement: Let G be a finite group and k a field. The group algebra k[G] is semisimple if and only if the characteristic of k does not divide the order of G.
proof_sketch: If char(k) ∤ |G|, prove every k[G]-module is completely reducible by averaging a linear projection over the group action using the Reynolds operator. The converse is shown by considering the sum of all group elements.

name: Noether Normalization Lemma
statement: Let A be a finitely generated algebra over a field k. Then there exist algebraically independent elements y₁,…,y_d ∈ A such that A is a finitely generated module over the polynomial subring k[y₁,…,y_d].
proof_sketch: Induct on the number of generators. If the generators are algebraically dependent, replace them to lower the total degree of the relation, eventually obtaining algebraically independent elements over which A is finite.

name: Cauchy's Theorem (Group Theory)
statement: If G is a finite group and p is a prime dividing the order of G, then G contains an element of order p.
proof_sketch: Consider the set of p-tuples of elements of G whose product is the identity. The cyclic group of order p acts on this set by cyclic permutation. The size of the set is |G|^(p-1), divisible by p. The fixed points under the action correspond to elements of order dividing p. By the class equation, the number of fixed points is congruent to the size of the set mod p, hence divisible by p. Since the identity is a fixed point, there must be nontrivial fixed points, giving an element of order p.

name: Trivial and Proper Subgroups
statement: The subgroup {1_G} is called the trivial subgroup (denoted 1). Any subgroup H ≠ G is a proper subgroup, written H < G.

name: Order of a Group
statement: The order of a group is the cardinality of its underlying set G, denoted |G|.

name: Generalized Associative Law
statement: If an element of a group is constructed from a sequence x₁, x₂, …, xₙ in order by repeatedly inserting brackets and applying the operation, the element must equal (…((x₁ ∘ x₂) ∘ x₃)…) ∘ xₙ, independently of the bracketing.
proof_sketch: Induction on n. Write u = v ∘ w with v from x₁…xᵢ, w from xᵢ₊₁…xₙ. If w = xₙ, induction applies directly. Else w = w' ∘ xₙ, then u = (v ∘ w') ∘ xₙ and induction gives the result.

name: Ω-Direct Factor and Indecomposability
statement: Let G be a group with operator domain Ω. An Ω-subgroup H is called an Ω-direct factor of G if there exists an Ω-subgroup K such that G = H × K; K is an Ω-direct complement of H. If G has no proper nontrivial Ω-direct factors, G is Ω-indecomposable (or indecomposable if Ω = ∅).
proof_sketch: None (definition).

name: Soluble Word Problem
statement: For a finite presentation, the word problem is soluble if there is an algorithm that, given a word w in the generators, decides whether w is a relator (i.e., w = 1 in the group). A group G has soluble word problem if the word problem is soluble for some (and hence any) finite presentation of G.

name: Definition of Group-Theoretical Class and Property
name_norm: definition of group-theoretical class and property
statement: A group-theoretical class X is a class of groups such that (i) X contains a group of order 1, and (ii) if G_1 ≅ G ∈ X then G_1 ∈ X. A group-theoretical property is a property of groups such that a group of order 1 has it, and if a group has it, so does any isomorphic copy. There is a natural bijection between group-theoretical properties and group-theoretical classes.

name: Sylow's Theorems
statement: Let G be a finite group of order p^a * m where p is prime and p does not divide m. Then (i) Every p-subgroup of G is contained in a subgroup of order p^a; in particular, Sylow p-subgroups always exist. (ii) If n_p is the number of Sylow p-subgroups, n_p ≡ 1 mod p. (iii) All Sylow p-subgroups are conjugate in G.
proof_sketch: Let X be the set of all subsets of G of size p^a. G acts on X by right multiplication. Show p does not divide |X| = C(p^a m, p^a) by examining powers of p in numerator and denominator. Hence there exists a G-orbit whose size is not divisible by p. For an element in this orbit, its stabilizer P has index not divisible by p, and its order divides p^a and is at least p^a, so |P|=p^a; thus a Sylow p-subgroup exists. For (ii), let P be a Sylow p-subgroup and let 𝒮 be the set of its conjugates. P acts on 𝒮 by conjugation; orbits have p-power size. If {P1} is an orbit of size 1, then P ≤ N_G(P1), PP1 is a p-group containing P, so P=P1. Thus {P} is the unique orbit of size 1, giving n_p ≡ 1 mod p. For (iii), if P2 is a p-subgroup not contained in any conjugate of P, then P2 acts on 𝒮; any P2-orbit would have size >1 (otherwise P2 ≤ P3), so all orbit sizes are divisible by p, making n_p ≡ 0 mod p, contradicting (ii). Hence every p-subgroup lies in a conjugate of P; in particular all Sylow p-subgroups are conjugate.

name: Metabelian Group
statement: A soluble group with derived length at most 2 is called metabelian.

name: Nilpotent Extension Property
statement: Let P be a property of groups that is closed under forming extensions. If G is a nilpotent group and its abelianization G_ab has P, then G itself has P.
proof_sketch: Set F_i = γ_i G / γ_{i+1} G. Assume F_i has P. Then F_{i+1}, as a homomorphic image of F_i ⊗ G_ab, also has P. By induction every lower central factor has P. Since G is nilpotent, some γ_{c+1} G = 1, so G is built by repeated extensions from these factors. As P is closed under extensions, G has P.

name: Direct Limit of a Direct System of Groups
name_norm: direct limit of a direct system of groups
statement: Given a direct system {G_λ, α_λ^μ} with all G_λ disjoint, define an equivalence relation ∼ on U = ⋃ G_λ by g_λ ∼ g_μ if α_λ^ν(g_λ) = α_μ^ν(g_μ) for some ν ≥ λ, μ. Let D be the set of equivalence classes [g_λ]. Define multiplication by [g_λ][g_μ] = [α_λ^ν(g_λ) α_μ^ν(g_μ)] for any ν ≥ λ, μ. Then D is a group, and the maps θ_λ: G_λ → D given by θ_λ(g_λ) = [g_λ] are homomorphisms. The pair (D, {θ_λ}) is the direct limit of the direct system.
proof_sketch: None

name: External Direct Product of Groups
statement: The subset of the cartesian product consisting of all (g_λ) such that g_λ = 1_λ for almost all λ (i.e., all but finitely many) is the external direct product D = Dr_{λ∈Λ} G_λ. If Λ is finite, D = C and is denoted G_{λ_1} × ··· × G_{λ_n}.

name: Induced Character
statement: If ρ has character χ, the character of the induced representation ρ^G is denoted χ^G and is called the induced character.

name: p-Normality Definition
statement: If P is a Sylow p-subgroup of G and if the center of P is weakly closed in P, then G is said to be p-normal. Among p-normal groups are groups with abelian Sylow p-subgroups and groups in which distinct Sylow p-subgroups have trivial intersection.
proof_sketch: None

name: Frattini subgroup
statement: The Frattini subgroup Frat G of a group G is the intersection of all maximal subgroups of G, with the convention Frat G = G if G has no maximal subgroups. It is a characteristic subgroup.
proof_sketch: Characteristicity follows from the fact that automorphisms permute maximal subgroups.

name: Definition of Ω-subgroup
statement: An Ω-subgroup of an Ω-group G is a subgroup H that is Ω-admissible, i.e., hʷ ∈ H for every h ∈ H and ω ∈ Ω.

name: Definition of Ω-homomorphism
statement: Let G and H be Ω-groups. An Ω-homomorphism is a homomorphism α: G → H such that (gʷ)ᵃ = (gᵃ)ʷ for all g ∈ G and ω ∈ Ω.

name: Structure of the Ω-subgroup generated by a set
name_norm: structure of the ω-subgroup generated by a set
statement: If G is an Ω-group and X a nonempty subset, the Ω-subgroup Xᴼ generated by X consists of all elements of the form (x_{i₁}^{ε₁})ʷ¹ … (x_{i_r}^{ε_r})ʷʳ, where x_i ∈ X, ε_i = ±1, r ≥ 0, and each ω_i is a finite sequence of operators from Ω applied successively.
proof_sketch: By the method of 1.3.3: the intersection of all Ω-subgroups containing X is the smallest such subgroup; these elements are contained in every Ω-subgroup containing X and themselves form an Ω-subgroup, hence they constitute Xᴼ.

name: Group Ring
statement: For a group G and a ring R with identity, the group ring RG is the set of all formal sums ∑_{x∈G} r_x x with r_x ∈ R and only finitely many r_x ≠ 0, with addition (∑ r_x x) + (∑ s_x x) = ∑ (r_x + s_x) x and multiplication (∑ r_x x)(∑ s_y y) = ∑_{x,y} r_x s_y (xy). It is a ring with identity 1_R 1_G.
proof_sketch: It is very simple to verify that with these rules RG satisfies the ring axioms.

name: Existence and Uniqueness of Maximal Normal Centerless CR-subgroup
name_norm: existence and uniqueness of maximal normal centerless cr-subgroup
statement: In any group G there exists a unique maximal normal centerless CR-subgroup. Moreover, this subgroup is characteristic in G.
proof_sketch: Consider a chain of normal centerless CR-subgroups M_α. A simple direct factor S of some M_α is subnormal in the union J, so S ⊲ J. Since J is generated by normal nonabelian simple subgroups, J is a CR-group by 3.3.10. Zorn's Lemma gives a maximal such subgroup M. For any other N, let I = M ∩ N. By 3.3.12, M = M_1 × I with M_1 ⊲ G. Then MN = M_1N = M_1 × N is a centerless CR-group. By maximality N ≤ M, proving uniqueness. Characteristicity follows from uniqueness under automorphisms.

name: Cyclic direct summand lemma for bounded abelian p-groups
name_norm: cyclic direct summand lemma for bounded abelian p-groups
statement: Let G be an abelian p-group whose elements have bounded orders and let g be an element of maximal order in G. Then ⟨g⟩ is a direct summand of G.
proof_sketch: By Zorn's Lemma choose M ≤ G maximal with M ∩ ⟨g⟩ = 0. If M + ⟨g⟩ ≠ G, pick x ∈ G ∖ (M + ⟨g⟩) of minimal order. Then px ∈ M + ⟨g⟩, leading to relations that contradict maximality of M or minimality of x. Hence G = M ⊕ ⟨g⟩.

name: Definition of Extra-Special p-Group
statement: A finite p-group G is called extra-special if G' and Z(G) coincide and have order p.

name: Standard Decomposition of Nondegenerate Skew-Symmetric Bilinear Forms
name_norm: standard decomposition of nondegenerate skew-symmetric bilinear forms
statement: Let V be a vector space over GF(p) with a nondegenerate skew-symmetric bilinear form f. Then V decomposes as a direct sum V = V_1 ⊕ ... ⊕ V_n of 2-dimensional subspaces V_i with bases {u_i, v_i} such that f(u_i, v_i) = 1, f(u_i, v_j) = 0 for i ≠ j, and f(u_i, u_j) = 0 = f(v_i, v_j) for all i, j.
proof_sketch: Standard linear algebra result; proof not given in text.

name: Conjugacy Classes and Centralizers
statement: The conjugation action g ↦ (x ↦ g^{-1}xg) partitions G into orbits called conjugacy classes. The stabilizer of x is the centralizer C_G(x) = {g∈G | gx = xg}. The size of the conjugacy class of x is |G : C_G(x)|.
proof_sketch: N/A

name: Existence of Sylow p-subgroups via Zorn's Lemma
name_norm: existence of sylow p-subgroups via zorns lemma
statement: Every p-subgroup of a group G is contained in a Sylow p-subgroup; hence Sylow p-subgroups always exist.
proof_sketch: Consider the set of all p-subgroups of G containing a given p-subgroup, partially ordered by inclusion. Every chain has an upper bound (the union), so Zorn's Lemma yields a maximal element, which is a Sylow p-subgroup.

name: Hall's Residual Finiteness for Finitely Generated Abelian-by-Nilpotent Groups
name_norm: halls residual finiteness for finitely generated abelian-by-nilpotent groups
statement: Let G be a finitely generated group with a normal abelian subgroup A such that G/A is nilpotent. Then G is residually finite.
proof_sketch: Take non-identity g ∈ G. By Zorn's Lemma there is a normal subgroup N maximal not containing g. It suffices to show G/N is finite; assume N=1. Every nontrivial normal subgroup contains g. There is a unique smallest nontrivial normal subgroup M. If A=1, G is nilpotent, M is central of prime order p, and M lies in all nontrivial normal subgroups, so G is a p-group, hence finite. If A≠1, M ≤ A. M is a simple Z(G/A)-module, finite by 15.4.4. C = C_G(M) has finite index. By 15.3.6 there is n with γ_n(C) ∩ M = 1; since M ≤ ζC, γ_n(C)=1, so C is nilpotent. C is finitely generated because |G:C| is finite, so ζC is finitely generated abelian. Some power (ζC)^m is torsion-free, but intersects M trivially, forcing (ζC)^m = 1, so C is finite, hence G is finite.

name: Refinement to an Alpha-Composition Series
statement: Every a-series can be refined to an a-composition series.
proof_sketch: Consider the set of all refinements of the given a-series, partially ordered by refinement. For any chain, construct an upper bound by taking unions of lower terms and intersections of upper terms. Zorn's Lemma yields a maximal refinement, which is an a-composition series.

name: Higman Embedding Theorem
statement: A finitely generated group embeds into a finitely presented group if and only if it is recursively presented.
proof_sketch: Simulate the recursive enumeration of relations within a finitely presented group using HNN-extensions and free products.

name: Definition of inner derivation (1-coboundary)
statement: For a ∈ A, define a function δ(a): G → A by g^{δ(a)} = [g,a] = a^{-g+1}. Such derivations are called inner derivations (or 1-coboundaries). The set of inner derivations is denoted Inn(G,A) or B^1(G,A).
proof_sketch: The commutator identity [g_1 g_2, a] = [g_1, a]^{g_2}[g_2, a] shows δ(a) is a derivation.

name: Free Resolution
statement: An exact sequence 0 → R → F → A → 0 of Abelian groups in which F and R are free is called a free resolution of A.
proof_sketch: As a subgroup of a free group, R is also free (Jacobson), so such resolutions exist.

name: Singular Cochain Complex
statement: A singular q-cochain on a topological space X is a linear functional on the Z-module S_q(X) of singular q-chains. The group of singular q-cochains is s^q(X) = Hom(S_q(X), Z). The coboundary operator d is defined by (dw)(c) = w(∂c). The graded group S*(X) = ⊕ s^q(X) becomes a differential complex; the homology of this complex is the singular cohomology of X with integer coefficients, denoted H*(X). Replacing Z with an Abelian group G yields singular cohomology with coefficients in G, H*(X; G).
proof_sketch: Definition; no proof required.

name: Proposition 15.2: Short Exact Sequence for Two Open Sets
name_norm: short exact sequence for two open sets
statement: Let X = U ∪ V be the union of two open sets. There is a short exact sequence of singular chains: 0 → S_q(U ∩ V) → S_q(U) ⊕ S_q(V) → S^U_q(X) → 0.
proof_sketch: Proof referenced but not provided in this excerpt.

name: Section, Frame, and Space of Sections
name_norm: section, frame, and space of sections
statement: A section over V is a map s: V → E with π ∘ s = id_V. The space of all sections is Γ(V, E). A frame is a collection of sections s₁,...,sₙ that form a basis of each fiber. A global zero section exists.

name: Transition Functions and Cocycle Condition
statement: Transition functions g_{αβ} of a vector bundle satisfy the cocycle condition g_{αβ} ∘ g_{βγ} = g_{αγ} on V_α ∩ V_β ∩ V_γ.

name: Bundle Map Definition
statement: A bundle map (homomorphism) between vector bundles E and E' is a fiber-preserving smooth map f: E → E' that is linear on corresponding fibers.

name: Jacobi Symbol Definition
statement: Let b be a positive odd integer with prime factorization b = p1 p2 ... pm, where the pi are not necessarily distinct. The Jacobi symbol (a/b) is defined as (a/b) = (a/p1)(a/p2)...(a/pm), where (a/pi) is the Legendre symbol.

name: Cubic Gauss sum cubed
statement: If χ is a cubic character, then g(χ)^3 = p J(χ,χ).
proof_sketch: Special case of Proposition 8.3.3 with n = 3. The product on the right has only one term J(χ,χ), and χ(-1) = 1 because χ is a cubic character.

name: Quadratic Number Field
statement: An algebraic number field F is called a quadratic number field if [F : Q] = 2.

name: Algebraic Variety
statement: An algebraic set V defined by polynomials f1,...,fm is called an algebraic variety if the ideal generated by f1,...,fm in F[x1,...,xn] is a prime ideal.
proof_sketch: None

name: Algebraic Number Field and its Ring of Integers
name_norm: algebraic number field and its ring of integers
statement: An algebraic number field is a subfield F of the complex numbers with [F:ℚ] finite. The ring of algebraic integers in F is D = F ∩ Ω, where Ω is the set of all algebraic integers.

name: Clearing Denominators for Algebraic Numbers
statement: If β ∈ F, there is a nonzero integer b such that bβ ∈ D.
proof_sketch: β satisfies a₀βⁿ + a₁βⁿ⁻¹ + … + aₙ = 0 with aᵢ∈ℤ, a₀≠0. Multiply by a₀ⁿ⁻¹ to see a₀β is an algebraic integer.

name: Dedekind Ring
statement: A Dedekind ring is an integral domain in which every nonzero ideal can be written uniquely as a product of prime ideals.

name: Biquadratic reciprocity for primary elements with coprime coordinates
name_norm: biquadratic reciprocity for primary elements with coprime coordinates
proof_sketch: From the congruences cπ ≡ ae+bd (mod λ) and aπ ≡ ae+bd (mod π) one obtains χ_λ(c)χ_λ(π) = χ_λ(ae+bd) and χ_π(a)χ_π(λ) = χ_π(ae+bd). Multiplying the first by the conjugate of the second yields the reciprocity relation, with the sign factor arising from computing the terms on the right when a, c, ae+bd are nonunits.

name: Definition of f_p for an elliptic curve
name_norm: definition of f_p for an elliptic curve
statement: For the elliptic curve E : y² = x³ − Ax − B and a prime p, define f_p = − ∑_{x=0}^{p-1} ((x³ − Ax − B)/p).
proof_sketch: None (definition).

name: Definition of Hecke characters (Grossencharaktere)
statement: Characters introduced by Hecke, originally called 'Grossencharaktere'; in later terminology often called algebraic Hecke characters or characters of type A₀.
proof_sketch: None (definition).

name: Weil's definition of local and global zeta functions
name_norm: weils definition of local and global zeta functions
statement: In his 1954 paper, Weil defines local and global zeta functions for a nonsingular algebraic variety defined over an algebraic number field.
proof_sketch: None (definition).

name: Existence of ideal prime to a fixed ideal in every ideal class
name_norm: existence of ideal prime to a fixed ideal in every ideal class
statement: Let K be an algebraic number field, D its ring of integers, and M ⊂ D a fixed ideal. Then every ideal class in K contains an ideal that is relatively prime to M.
proof_sketch: Given an ideal class represented by A, construct an ideal C with (C, M) = 1 and A⁻¹ = C. Let {P₁, P₂, ..., P_t} be the primes dividing M but not A. For each prime P dividing A, let a(P) = ord_P(A). Choose π(P) ∈ P^{a(P)} \ P^{a(P)+1}. Use the Chinese Remainder Theorem to find α ∈ D such that α ≡ π(P) (mod P^{a(P)+1}) for P|A, and α ≡ 1 (mod P_i) for i = 1,...,t. Then (α) = A C with (C, M) = 1, so the inverse class of A contains C prime to M. Consequently every ideal class contains an ideal prime to M.

name: Gelfond-Schneider Theorem
statement: If α and β are algebraic numbers with α ≠ 0, 1 and β irrational, then α^β is transcendental.
proof_sketch: Using the method of Siegel–Shidlovsky or Gel'fond's original approach: assume algebraic and construct an auxiliary function to derive a contradiction by bounding derivatives and using arithmetic properties.

name: Hecke Operator
statement: For a positive integer n, the n-th Hecke operator T_n acts on modular forms of weight k by averaging over representatives of integral matrices of determinant n.
proof_sketch: Definition via double coset decomposition: T_n f(z) = n^{k-1} ∑_{ad=n, 0≤b<d} d^{-k} f((az+b)/d). One checks it preserves modularity.

name: Liouville's Theorem (Diophantine Approximation)
statement: If α is an algebraic number of degree d>1, there exists a constant c(α)>0 such that for all rational p/q, |α - p/q| > c(α) q^(-d).
proof_sketch: Let f be the minimal polynomial of α. By the mean value theorem, |f(p/q)| ≤ |α-p/q| max|f'|. Since f(p/q) is a nonzero rational with denominator at most q^d, its absolute value is at least 1/q^d, giving the bound.

name: Non-Archimedean Absolute Value (Valuation)
statement: An absolute value satisfying the stronger condition AV 4. |x + y|_v ≤ max(|x|_v, |y|_v) is called a valuation or non-archimedean.

name: Dependence of Absolute Values
statement: Two non-trivial absolute values are called dependent if they define the same topology; if they do not, they are independent.

name: Corollary on S-units modulo roots of unity
name_norm: corollary on s-units modulo roots of unity
statement: Let k be a number field and S a finite subset of M_k containing the archimedean absolute values. Then k_s modulo the group of roots of unity in k is a free abelian group on s − 1 generators.
proof_sketch: The kernel of the log map is the group of roots of unity in k (elements with all absolute values bounded form a finite group). The image is a free abelian group on s − 1 generators; the exact sequence yields the result.

name: Schwartz Space is a Vector Space
name_norm: schwartz space is a vector space
statement: The Schwartz space S is a vector space over the complex numbers C.
proof_sketch: Clear from the definition; the sum of two rapidly decaying C^∞ functions and scalar multiples remain rapidly decaying C^∞.

name: Partial Differential Operator DP on Schwartz Space
name_norm: partial differential operator dp on schwartz space
statement: For an n-tuple of non-negative integers P = (p1,...,pn), D^P = ∂^{|p|}/∂x1^{p1}...∂xn^{pn} where |p| = p1+...+pn. The operator D^P maps S into itself.
proof_sketch: By definition of S, partial derivatives of all orders exist, are continuous, and tend to 0 rapidly at infinity.

name: Multiplication Operator MP on Schwartz Space
name_norm: multiplication operator mp on schwartz space
statement: M_j is multiplication by the j-th variable. M^P f(x) = x1^{p1}...xn^{pn} f(x). The operator M^P maps S into itself.
proof_sketch: Multiplication by a polynomial preserves smoothness, and the product of a polynomial with a rapidly decaying function remains rapidly decaying.

name: Differentiation Under the Integral Sign
statement: For suitable functions K(x,y) (e.g., Schwartz functions in x uniformly in y), ∂/∂y_j ∫ K(x,y) dx = ∫ ∂/∂y_j K(x,y) dx, provided the partial derivatives of K exist, are continuous, and bounded by an absolutely integrable function of x over R^n, independent of y.
proof_sketch: Apply the mean value theorem to the difference quotient. Split the integral into a finite region and a tail. The tail is made small using the absolute integrability of the derivative bound. On the finite region, uniform continuity of the derivative forces the difference between the difference quotient and the derivative to be small as h → 0.

name: Artin Reciprocity Law
statement: Let K/k be an abelian extension. There exists an admissible cycle c and a subgroup H of the ideal group I(c) containing the principal ideals P_c such that the Artin symbol p → (p, K/k) induces an isomorphism I(c)/H ≅ Gal(K/k), extended to all fractional ideals by multiplicativity.
proof_sketch: Not provided in this excerpt; assumed known.

name: Conductor of a p-adic Quasi-Character
statement: For a p-adic field, the subgroups 1 + ℘ᵐ (m ≥ 0) form a fundamental system of neighborhoods of 1 in U. Any quasi-character c vanishes on some 1 + ℘ᵐ. The conductor 𝔣 of c is the ideal 𝔣 = ℘ᵐ where m is the smallest integer such that c(1 + ℘ᵐ) = 1. If m = 0, then 𝔣 = 0.
proof_sketch: Since c is continuous and trivial on an open neighborhood of 1, and the subgroups 1+℘ᵐ form a basis, there exists a minimal m (or 0).

name: Characterisation of I via truncation I'
name_norm: characterisation of i via truncation i
statement: Let I be a homogeneous ideal, and let I' = I ∩ (Z₀,…,Zₙ)^k (the truncation). Then a polynomial F lies in I if and only if the product of F with every homogeneous polynomial of sufficiently high degree lies in I'.
proof_sketch: F ∈ I implies its homogeneous components are in I, so multiplying by high-degree monomials pushes them into I'. Conversely, if F·G ∈ I' ⊂ I for all high-degree G, using Noetherianity one can show F ∈ I; the text gives the idea via the saturation.

name: Ceiling-Floor Identity
proof_sketch: By definition, ⌊-x⌋ is the largest integer ≤ -x, so -⌊-x⌋ is the smallest integer ≥ x, which is exactly ⌈x⌉.

name: Nearest Integer Function
statement: ⌊x⌋ denotes an integer nearest to x, defined by ⌊x⌋ = ⌊x + 1/2⌋.
proof_sketch: None

name: Divisibility Notations
statement: d|n means d divides n; d||n means d|n and (d, n/d) = 1; notations p|n and p^a||n imply p is prime, with p^a||n meaning p^a is the highest power of p dividing n.
proof_sketch: None

name: Signature of a Number Field
statement: The signature of a number field is the pair (r1, r2) where r1 is the number of embeddings of K whose image lie in R, and 2r2 is the number of non-real complex embeddings, so that r1 + 2r2 = n. Non-real embeddings come in complex conjugate pairs. If T is an irreducible polynomial defining K by one of its roots, r1 (resp. 2r2) is the number of real (resp. non-real) roots of T in C. When r2 = 0 (resp. r1 = 0) the field and polynomial are called totally real (resp. totally complex).

name: Positive Definite Quadratic Forms as Gram Matrices
name_norm: positive definite quadratic forms as gram matrices
statement: (1) If Q is the matrix of a positive definite quadratic form, then Q is the Gram matrix of some lattice basis, i.e. there exists a matrix B ∈ GL_n(R) such that Q = B^t B. (2) The Gram matrix of a lattice basis b_i determines this basis uniquely up to isometry. In other words, if the b_i and the b'_i have the same Gram matrix, then the b'_i can be obtained from the b_i by an orthogonal transformation. In matrix terms, B' = K B where K is an orthogonal matrix.
proof_sketch: Take B as the coordinate matrix of a basis on an orthonormal basis of E; then Q = B^t B. For uniqueness up to isometry, if two bases have the same Gram matrix, their coordinate matrices satisfy B'^t B' = B^t B, so (B' B^{-1})^t (B' B^{-1}) = I, hence B' = K B with K orthogonal.

name: Definition of unit, divisibility, and irreducible element in an integral domain
name_norm: definition of unit, divisibility, and irreducible element in an integral domain
statement: Let R be an integral domain. An element u in R is a unit if it has a multiplicative inverse in R. For a, b in R with b ≠ 0, we say that b divides a (written b | a) if there exists q ∈ R such that a = bq; such a q is unique and denoted a/b. An element p ∈ R is called irreducible (or prime) if whenever q divides p, either q or p/q is a unit.

name: Fundamental Discriminant
statement: An integer D is called a fundamental discriminant if D is the discriminant of a quadratic field K. Equivalently, D ≠ 1 and either D ≡ 1 (mod 4) and is squarefree, or D ≡ 0 (mod 4), D/4 is squarefree and D/4 ≡ 2 or 3 (mod 4).
proof_sketch: N/A

name: Unit Group and Torsion Subgroup
statement: The set of units in a number field K form a multiplicative group denoted U(K). The torsion subgroup of U(K), consisting of the roots of unity in K, is denoted μ(K).

name: Definition of the Arithmetic-Geometric Mean (AGM)
statement: Let a and b be two positive real numbers. The Arithmetic-Geometric mean of a and b, denoted by AGM(a,b), is defined as the common limit of the sequences a_n and b_n defined by a_0 = a, b_0 = b, a_{n+1} = (a_n + b_n)/2 and b_{n+1} = √(a_n b_n).

name: Sum of Character Values
statement: Let χ be a Dirichlet character modulo q. (i) If χ ≠ χ₀, then ∑_{x∈(ℤ/qℤ)^*} χ(x) = 0. (ii) Dually, if x ≢ 1 (mod q), then ∑_{χ} χ(x) = 0, where the sum runs over all Dirichlet characters modulo q.
proof_sketch: For (i), choose a with χ(a) ≠ 1 and let S = ∑_x χ(x); then χ(a)S = ∑_x χ(ax) = S, so (χ(a)-1)S = 0, hence S=0. For (ii), apply the same argument to the dual group of characters.

name: Quotient Field of an Integral Domain
name_norm: quotient field of an integral domain
statement: The quotient field K(R) of an integral domain R is the localization R[U^{-1}] where U = R \ {0}.

name: Zero in localizations
statement: a. If m ∈ M, then m = 0 if and only if m goes to zero in each localization M_m at a maximal ideal m of R. b. M = 0 if and only if M_m = 0 for each maximal ideal m of R.
proof_sketch: a. The annihilator I of m is not contained in m iff m becomes zero in M_m. m=0 iff I = R iff I is contained in no maximal ideal (using Zorn's lemma). b. Apply a to every element of M.

name: Definition of Normal Domain
statement: An integral domain R is normal if it is integrally closed in its fraction field.

name: General Nullstellensatz
statement: Let R be a Jacobson ring. If S is a finitely generated R-algebra, then S is a Jacobson ring. Moreover, if n ⊂ S is a maximal ideal, then m = n ∩ R is a maximal ideal of R, and S/n is a finite extension field of R/m.
proof_sketch: The proof proceeds by reducing to the case S = R[x] using Lemma 4.20 and induction on the number of generators. Given a prime P of R[x] and a nonzero b in (R[x])/P with ((R[x])/P)[b⁻¹] a field, one shows (R[x])/P is a field. For the second statement, S/n is a field; its subring R/m is a domain integral over a field, hence a field, and the finite generation makes S/n a finite extension.

name: Rabinowitch's Trick (Jacobson Criterion)
statement: For a ring R, the following are equivalent: (a) R is a Jacobson ring. (b) If P is a prime ideal of R and S = R/P contains a nonzero element b such that S[b⁻¹] is a field, then S is a field.
proof_sketch: (a) ⇒ (b): R Jacobson implies S is Jacobson, so the intersection of maximal ideals of S is zero. In S[b⁻¹], primes correspond to primes of S not containing b. Since S[b⁻¹] is a field, b lies in every nonzero prime ideal of S, so (0) is maximal and S is a field. (b) ⇒ (a): Let Q be prime, I the intersection of maximal ideals containing Q. If I ≠ Q, pick x ∈ I \ Q and apply (b) to S = R/Q with b = x + Q. One shows S[x⁻¹] is a field, forcing S to be a field and contradicting that Q is not maximal.

name: Family as Morphism
statement: A family of varieties or algebras is a morphism; if φ: X → B is a morphism of varieties, the preimages of points of B (called fibers) are varieties that vary in a family parametrized by the points of B.

name: Radical of Annihilator and Properties of Parameter Ideals
name_norm: radical of annihilator and properties of parameter ideals
statement: Let M be a finitely generated module over a ring R, and q an ideal. Then rad(ann(M/qM)) = rad(q + ann M). In particular, if (R, m) is local: (a) q is a parameter ideal for M iff q + ann M ⊇ m^n for some n ≫ 0 iff q is a parameter ideal for R/(ann M); (b) for a short exact sequence 0 → M' → M → M'' → 0, q is a parameter ideal for M iff it is a parameter ideal for both M' and M''; (c) dim M is the minimal number of generators of a parameter ideal for M.
proof_sketch: To prove the radical equality, by Corollary 2.12 it is enough that a prime P contains ann(M/qM) iff P ⊇ q + ann M. By Proposition 2.1, P ⊇ ann(M/qM) iff (M/qM)_P ≠ 0. Nakayama's lemma gives (M/qM)_P = M_P/q_P M_P ≠ 0 iff M_P ≠ 0 and q_P ⊆ P_P. By Proposition 2.1 this holds iff P ⊇ ann M and P ⊇ q, i.e. P ⊇ q + ann M. Part (a) follows because rad(q+ann M)=m iff it contains a power of m, and ann(R/(ann M)/q(R/(ann M))) = q+ann M. Part (b) uses the induced exact sequence M'/qM' → M/qM → M''/qM'' → 0; finite length lifts from M' and M'' to M. Part (c): dim M = dim R/(ann M), so Corollary 10.7 via (a) yields the minimal number of generators.

name: Discrete valuation ring (DVR)
statement: A discrete valuation ring (DVR) is a one-dimensional regular local ring; equivalently, the valuation ring of a discrete valuation.

name: Dimension equals transcendence degree for affine domains
name_norm: dimension equals transcendence degree for affine domains
statement: If R is an affine domain over a field k, then dim R = tr.deg_k R.
proof_sketch: Let d = dim R. By Noether normalization (Theorem 13.3), there exists a polynomial subring S = k[X₁, …, X_d] over which R is finite. Then tr.deg_k S = d, and since R is finite over S, tr.deg_k R = tr.deg_k S = d.

name: Artinian Property of Monomial Orders
statement: Let F be a free S-module with basis. Any monomial order on F is Artinian (every subset has a least element).
proof_sketch: If X is a set of monomials of F, the submodule generated by X is generated by a finite subset Y (since S Noetherian). The least element of Y is the least element of X because every element of X is a multiple of an element of Y.

name: Extension of Monomial Order to Terms
name_norm: extension of monomial order to terms
statement: If um and vn are terms with non-zero coefficients u, v in k, and m, n are monomials with m > n (resp. m ≥ n) then we say um > vn (resp. um ≥ vn). This is not a partial order but a convenient notation.

name: Initial Submodule
statement: For a monomial order > and a submodule M of F, in>(M) is the monomial submodule generated by {in>(f) : f ∈ M}.

name: R-linear Derivation
statement: If S is an R-algebra, a derivation d: S → M is R-linear if it is a map of R-modules. The set of all R-linear derivations from S to M is denoted Der_R(S, M).

name: Vanishing of Derivation on 1
statement: For any derivation d: S → M, d(1) = 0.

name: Dimension Formula for Polynomial Rings
statement: a) If k is a field, dim k[x₁,…,xᵣ] = r. b) For any ring R, dim R[x] = 1 + dim R. c) If P is a prime ideal of R, there exist prime ideals Q of R[x] contracting to P, and for a maximal such Q, dim R[x]Q = 1 + dim R_P.
proof_sketch: Part a follows from b by induction. For b, any chain P₁ ⊂ … ⊂ P_d in R yields a chain P₁R[x] ⊂ … ⊂ P_dR[x] ⊂ P_dR[x]+(x), giving dim R[x] ≥ 1+dim R. The reverse inequality follows from part c. For part c, first treat R a field (P=0): R[x] is a PID, maximal Q has codimension 1. In general, localize at P to assume R local with maximal ideal P; any maximal Q containing P contracts to P. Apply Theorem 10.10 to obtain codim Q = 1 + dim R.

name: Depth Lemma (Lemma 18.3)
statement: Let (R, P) be a local ring, M a finitely generated R-module, I an ideal of R, and y ∈ P. Then depth((I, y), M) ≤ depth(I, M) + 1.
proof_sketch: Let x1,...,xn generate I, set r = depth((I,y),M). By Theorem 17.4, Hi(M ⊗ K(x1,...,xn,y)) = 0 for i < r. By Corollary 17.11 and Nakayama, Hi(M ⊗ K(x1,...,xn)) = 0 for i < r-1. Theorem 17.4 then gives depth(I, M) ≥ r-1, so r ≤ depth(I,M)+1.

name: Depth of an ideal on a module
name_norm: depth of an ideal on a module
statement: Let I be an ideal of R and M a finitely generated R-module. If IM ≠ M, define the depth of I on M, written depth(I, M), to be the length of any maximal M-sequence in I. If IM = M, set depth(I, M) = ∞. When M = R, write depth(I) for depth(I, R). If R is local with maximal ideal P, the depth of M is depth(P, M).
proof_sketch: By Theorem 17.4, all maximal M-sequences in I have the same length whenever IM ≠ M, so depth(I, M) is well-defined.

name: Locality and Completion of Cohen–Macaulay Rings (Proposition 18.8)
name_norm: locality and completion of cohen–macaulay rings (proposition 18.8
statement: R is Cohen–Macaulay iff R_P is Cohen–Macaulay for every maximal ideal P of R, and then R_Q is Cohen–Macaulay for every prime Q of R. A local ring is Cohen–Macaulay iff its completion is Cohen–Macaulay.
proof_sketch: If R is CM and Q is prime, codim Q_Q = codim Q = depth(Q,R) ≤ depth Q_Q ≤ codim Q_Q by Proposition 18.2, so equality holds and R_Q is CM. If R_P is CM for all maximal P, Lemma 18.1 gives depth(P,R)=depth(P_P,R_P) and codim P = codim P_P, so R is CM. For local (R,P) with completion (R̂,P̂), codim P = codim P̂. If x₁,…,xₙ generate P, the Koszul complex over R̂ is K̂ = R̂ ⊗ K(x₁,…,xₙ). Flatness (Theorem 7.2b) and Nakayama (Theorem 7.2a) ensure nonzero finitely generated R-modules remain nonzero after tensoring, so depth(P,R)=depth(P̂,R̂) by Theorem 17.4.

name: Koszul complex (for a map to R)
name_norm: koszul complex (for a map to r
statement: The Koszul complex is associated to a sequence of elements of R, or equivalently to a map from a finitely generated free R-module F to R.
proof_sketch: Standard construction using exterior powers; not detailed here.

name: Boundaries, Cycles, and Homology
statement: In a complex F, the elements of the image of φ_{i+1} are called boundaries, the elements of the kernel of φ_i are called cycles. The homology of F at F_i, denoted H_i(F), is the quotient module (ker φ_i) / (im φ_{i+1}).

name: Module as a Complex
statement: An R-module M may be considered as a complex with only one nonzero term (all other terms zero).

name: Limit of a Spectral Sequence
statement: Given a spectral sequence, define submodules 0 = 1B ⊂ 2B ⊂ … ⊂ rB ⊂ … ⊂ rZ ⊂ … ⊂ 2Z ⊂ 1Z = 1E such that iE = iZ / iB. Let 1Z = 1E, 1B = 0. Having defined iB and iZ for i ≤ r, define r+1Z as the kernel of the composite map rZ → rZ/rB = rE → rE, and r+1B as the image of this map. Then set 00Z = ∩r rZ, 00B = ∪r rB. The limit is 00E = 00Z / 00B.
proof_sketch: None (construction/definition).

name: Uniqueness of colimits up to isomorphism
name_norm: uniqueness of colimits up to isomorphism
statement: If a colimit of a diagram F exists, it is unique up to a unique isomorphism compatible with the cocone maps.
proof_sketch: If (A, ψ) and (A', ψ') are colimits, the universal property yields unique maps γ: A → A' and γ': A' → A with γ∘ψ_B = ψ'_B and γ'∘ψ'_B = ψ_B. Then γ'∘γ: A → A satisfies (γ'∘γ)∘ψ_B = ψ_B, as does the identity id_A. Uniqueness in the universal property forces γ'∘γ = id_A. Similarly γ∘γ' = id_A', so γ is an isomorphism.

name: Coproduct as a colimit
statement: The coproduct of a collection of objects {B_i} of A, denoted ∐_i B_i, is the colimit of the diagram consisting of the objects B_i together with only their identity morphisms.
proof_sketch: None (definition).

name: Coequalizer as a colimit
statement: The coequalizer of a pair of parallel morphisms ψ, ψ': C_1 → C_2 in A is the colimit of the diagram consisting of the objects C_1, C_2, their identity morphisms, and the two morphisms ψ, ψ'.
proof_sketch: None (definition).

name: Colimit as a functor
statement: If A has all colimits of diagrams of shape B, then colim defines a functor lim: Fun(B, A) → A from the category of functors (with natural transformations) sending a diagram F to its colimit and a natural transformation α: F → G to the unique morphism lim α: lim F → lim G induced by the composite cocone F(B) → G(B) → lim G.
proof_sketch: For a natural transformation α: F → G, the maps F(B) → G(B) → lim G form a cocone from F to lim G. The universal property of lim F gives a unique map lim α: lim F → lim G. Functoriality follows from uniqueness of the induced map.

name: Syzygy Modules
statement: Given an exact sequence 0 → N → F → M → 0 with F projective, N is called a first syzygy module of M. The nth syzygy module is defined inductively as the first syzygy module of the (n−1)st syzygy module. Syzygy modules are unique up to projective summands.
proof_sketch: N/A

name: Bounded-below complexes
statement: K+(M) is the full subcategory of K(M) consisting of bounded-below complexes F such that F_i = 0 for i sufficiently small.
proof_sketch: Definition by restriction.

name: Homology is well-defined on K(M)
statement: The nth homology module H_n(X) is well-defined for any object X in K(M), because homotopic maps induce identical maps on homology.
proof_sketch: If f ~ g, then f - g = hd + dh, so H(f) = H(g). Thus homology depends only on the homotopy class.

name: Minimal Injective Resolution
statement: An injective resolution ℰ of M is minimal if each E^n is the injective hull of ker(E^n → E^{n+1}).
proof_sketch: None (definition).

name: Five-Term Exact Sequence (Tor)
statement: If 0 → A → B → C → 0 is a short exact sequence of modules and M is a module, then there is an exact sequence Tor_1(A,M) → Tor_1(B,M) → Tor_1(C,M) → A⊗M → B⊗M → C⊗M → 0.
proof_sketch: Take a projective resolution of M and tensor with the short exact sequence; the resulting exact sequence of complexes gives a long exact sequence in homology, whose low-degree terms yield the five-term exact sequence.

name: Graded structure on the tensor algebra of a graded module
name_norm: graded structure on the tensor algebra of a graded module
statement: If R is a graded ring concentrated in degree 0 and M = ⊕ M_i is a Z-graded R-module, then T_R(M) is Z-graded with i-th graded component ⊕_{k} ⊕_{i_1+⋯+i_k = i} M_{i_1} ⊗_R ⋯ ⊗_R M_{i_k}.

name: Algebraic Independence and Transcendence Basis
statement: A subset B of a field extension K/k is algebraically independent over k if the subfield k(B) is isomorphic to the rational function field k({X_b}_{b∈B}) via the map sending X_b to b. Equivalently, for any integer n, any nonzero polynomial f(t_1,…,t_n) with coefficients in k, and any distinct b_1,…,b_n ∈ B, f(b_1,…,b_n) ≠ 0. B is a transcendence basis for K over k if B is algebraically independent and K is algebraic over k(B).

name: Matroid Basis Cardinality Lemma
statement: If B is a matroid, then all bases in B have the same cardinality.
proof_sketch: Let r be the minimal cardinality among bases. For a basis B, prove by downward induction on the number of elements B shares with a basis B' of size r. If they share r elements, B = B' since no basis contains another. If they share s < r elements, choose b' ∈ B' \ B. By the exchange property, there is a basis (B' \ {b'}) ∪ {b} of size r sharing s+1 elements with B.

name: Absolute Convergence of Local Height Series on $E(\mathbb{R})$
name_norm: absolute convergence of local height series on $e(\mathbb{r})$
statement: For an elliptic curve $E/\mathbb{R}$ given by a Weierstrass equation, there exist constants $C_1, C_2 > 0$ such that $C_1 \le \frac{|z([2^n]P)|}{|x([2^n]P)|^2} \le C_2$ for all $P \in E(\mathbb{R})$ and all $n$, and the series $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{4^n} \log|z([2^n]P)|$ is absolutely convergent.
proof_sketch: Exercise 6.9(b): bound $z([2]P)$ in terms of $x(P)$ and iterate.

name: Definition of Normal Scheme
statement: A scheme is normal if all its local rings are integrally closed.

name: Definition of Finite Type Morphism
statement: A morphism is of finite type if the extensions of local rings are finitely generated algebras.

name: Definition of Uniformizer at a Point
name_norm: definition of uniformizer at a point
statement: Fix a point p in Spec(R) with discrete valuation ord_p. A uniformizer u ∈ R for p is an element such that ord_p(u) = 1.

name: Vector Fields Agreeing on a Set
name_norm: vector fields agreeing on a set
statement: Two vector fields V and W agree on a set A if V(P) = W(P) for all P in A.
proof_sketch: None (definition).

name: Vector Field on a Sphere
statement: A vector field V on a sphere S assigns to each point P in S a vector V(P) in the tangent space T_pS, with the mapping P ↦ V(P) being continuous. For the standard sphere S^2 = {(x,y,z): x^2+y^2+z^2=1}, the tangent space is T_pS = {(a,b,c): (a,b,c)·(x,y,z) = ax+by+cz = 0}.
proof_sketch: None (definition).

name: Isomorphism of Coverings
statement: An isomorphism between coverings p: Y → X and p': Y' → X is a homeomorphism φ: Y → Y' such that p' ∘ φ = p.
proof_sketch: None (definition).

name: Reduced Zeroth Cohomology Group
statement: For a nonempty space U, the reduced 0th cohomology group H̃^0(U) is the quotient of the space of locally constant functions on U by the subspace of constant functions; thus dim H̃^0(U) = dim H^0(U) − 1, and U is connected iff H̃^0(U) = 0.
proof_sketch: None (definition).

name: Restriction of a Covering is a Covering
name_norm: restriction of a covering is a covering
statement: If p: Y → X is a covering and X' is any subspace of X, then the restriction p': Y' = p^{-1}(X') → X' is a covering.
proof_sketch: For x in X', let N be an evenly covered neighborhood in X; then N ∩ X' is evenly covered by the restriction.

name: Ramification Point
statement: A point P in X is called a ramification point for the analytic map f if its ramification index satisfies e_f(P) > 1.
proof_sketch: None (definition).

name: Poincaré Duality for Compact Oriented Manifolds
name_norm: poincaré duality for compact oriented manifolds
statement: If X is a compact oriented n-manifold, then the pairing H^k X × H^{n-k} X -> R defined by (ω, μ) ↦ ∫_X ω ∧ μ is a perfect pairing: for every linear functional φ on H^{n-k} X there exists a unique ω ∈ H^k X such that φ(μ) = ∫_X ω ∧ μ for all μ ∈ H^{n-k} X.
proof_sketch: Follows from the Duality Theorem and the nondegeneracy of the integration pairing.

name: Chain Complex of an Ordered Simplicial Complex
name_norm: chain complex of an ordered simplicial complex
statement: For an ordered simplicial complex K (partial order on vertices such that each simplex is totally ordered), CnK is the free abelian group on the n-simplices. The boundary ∂:CnK → Cn-1K is defined by ∂(v0,…,vn) = ∑_{i=0}^n (-1)^i (v0,…,v̂i,…,vn).
proof_sketch: None

name: Lemma C.10 (Surjectivity of φ*)
statement: If φ: A → A' is one-to-one, then φ*: Hom(A', ℝ) → Hom(A, ℝ) is surjective.
proof_sketch: Fix a maximal set {x_α} in A' such that no nontrivial integer linear combination lies in im(φ). For f ∈ Hom(A, ℝ) and any x ∈ A', choose n ≠ 0, y ∈ A with n x = Σ n_α x_α + φ(y). Define g(x) = (1/n) f(y). Show g is well-defined, a homomorphism, and φ*(g) = f.

name: Singular Cohomology Groups
statement: For a topological space X and abelian group G, the n-th singular cohomology group Hⁿ(X; G) is the quotient ker(δⁿ)/im(δⁿ⁻¹), where δ is the coboundary operator. Elements of ker are cocycles, elements of im are coboundaries.
proof_sketch: Verify that δⁿ⁺¹ ∘ δⁿ = 0 using ∂² = 0, so im(δⁿ⁻¹) ⊆ ker(δⁿ), thus the quotient is defined.

name: Vector Space Structure on a Field Extension
name_norm: vector space structure on a field extension
statement: For a field extension F ⊆ K, K is made into an F-vector space by defining scalar multiplication for α ∈ F and a ∈ K as α·a = αa (multiplication in K).

name: Finite and Infinite Extensions
statement: If [K : F] < ∞, then K is a finite extension of F; otherwise K is an infinite extension of F.

name: Field of Formal Generalized Power Series (Laurent Series)
name_norm: field of formal generalized power series (laurent series
statement: For a field k, k((x)) is the set of formal sums ∑_{n=n0}^∞ a_n x^n with n0 ∈ Z and a_n ∈ k. Addition is termwise and multiplication is convolution. The set k((x)) is a field with these operations.

name: Lemma 8.9: Number of F-homomorphisms Equals Separable Degree
name_norm: number of f-homomorphisms equals separable degree
statement: Let K be a finite extension of F, and let S be the separable closure of F in K. Then [S : F] is equal to the number of F-homomorphisms from K to an algebraic closure of F.
proof_sketch: Embed K in an algebraic closure M. By the primitive element theorem, S = F(α) for some α, with r = [S : F]. The minimal polynomial of α over F has r distinct roots α₁,…,αᵣ in M. The maps σᵢ: S → M defined by f(α) ↦ f(αᵢ) are exactly the r distinct F-homomorphisms from S to M. Since K/S is purely inseparable, K is normal over S, so each σᵢ extends to an F-homomorphism K → M.

name: Corollary 9.7: Intermediate Fields of Cyclic Kummer Extensions
name_norm: intermediate fields of cyclic kummer extensions
statement: Let K/F be a cyclic extension of degree n, and suppose F contains a primitive n-th root of unity. If K = F(n√a) with a ∈ F, then every intermediate field of K/F is of the form F(m√a) for some divisor m of n.
proof_sketch: Let σ generate Gal(K/F). Subgroups are ⟨σ^t⟩ for t|n; write n=tm. Then σ^t fixes a^m=(n√a)^m, so F(a^m) ⊆ Fix(σ^t). By Prop 9.6, order of a^m F* is m, so [F(a^m):F]=m. The Fundamental Theorem gives [Fix(σ^t):F]=m, thus they are equal.

name: Purely transcendental field extension
statement: A field K is purely transcendental over a subfield F if K is isomorphic to a field of rational functions over F in some number, finite or infinite, of variables. If K = F(t_1, ..., t_n) with {t_1, ..., t_n} algebraically independent, then K is often said to be a rational extension of F.

name: Radical Extensions are n-Radical
statement: If K = F(a1, ..., ar) is a radical extension with integers n1, ..., nr as in the definition, then K is an n-radical extension of F for n = n1 ··· nr.
proof_sketch: Since a_i ∈ F(a1, ..., a_{i-1}) and n is a multiple of each n_i, we have a_i^n ∈ F(a1, ..., a_{i-1}) for all i, satisfying the condition for an n-radical extension with uniform n.

name: Chain Characterization of Radical Extensions
statement: K is a radical extension of F if and only if there is a chain of fields F = F0 ⊆ F1 ⊆ ... ⊆ Fr = K such that F_{i+1} = F_i(a_i) for some a_i ∈ F_{i+1} with a_i^{n_i} ∈ F_i for some integer n_i.
proof_sketch: Forward direction: Set F_i = F(a1,...,a_i). Then F_{i+1} = F_i(a_{i+1}) with a_{i+1}^{n_{i+1}} ∈ F_i. Converse: If a chain exists, then a_i ∈ F_i, so K = F(a1,...,ar) and the n_i satisfy the radical extension condition.

name: Transitivity of Radical Extensions
statement: If K/F is a radical extension and L/K is a radical extension, then L/F is a radical extension.
proof_sketch: Let K = F(a1,...,ar) with a_i^{n_i} ∈ F(a1,...,a_{i-1}) and L = K(b1,...,bs) with b_j^{m_j} ∈ K(b1,...,b_{j-1}). Adjoining all a_i then all b_j gives a chain from F to L satisfying the radical condition. The combined chain yields a radical extension from F to L.

name: Characteristic of an Integral Domain
statement: If R is an integral domain, then its characteristic char(R) is either 0 or a prime number.
proof_sketch: Consider the map φ: Z → R sending n to n·1. Its image is a subring, and Z/ker(φ) is an integral domain, so ker(φ) is a prime ideal. Since ker(φ) is generated by char(R), char(R) must be 0 or prime.

name: Prime implies Irreducible
statement: If a is a prime element in an integral domain R, then a is irreducible.
proof_sketch: Suppose a = bc. Then a divides bc. Since a is prime, a divides b or a divides c. If a|b, then b = ad, so a = adc, implying dc = 1, so c is a unit. Similarly, if a|c, then b is a unit. Hence a is irreducible.

name: Irreducibles are Prime in a UFD
name_norm: irreducibles are prime in a ufd
statement: If R is a UFD and π ∈ R is irreducible, then π is prime.
proof_sketch: Suppose π divides ab, so ab = πc. Factor c into irreducibles: c = θ₁...θ_k. Then πθ₁...θ_k is a factorization of ab into irreducibles. By unique factorization, the irreducible π must appear (up to associates) in the factorization of a or b. Thus π divides a or π divides b.

name: Determination of F-automorphisms by generators
statement: Let K = F(X) be a field extension of F generated by a subset X of K. If σ, τ ∈ Gal(K/F) satisfy σ|_X = τ|_X, then σ = τ. Thus, F-automorphisms of K are uniquely determined by their action on a generating set.
proof_sketch: Any a ∈ K can be written as a rational function f(x_1,…,x_n)/g(x_1,…,x_n) with x_i ∈ X and coefficients in F. Since σ, τ fix F and respect addition/multiplication, applying them to this expression yields the same result if they agree on X.

name: Real square roots and odd degree polynomials have roots
name_norm: real square roots and odd degree polynomials have roots
statement: 1. If f(x) = x^2 - a for some a > 0, then f has a root in ℝ. Therefore, every nonnegative real number has a real square root. 2. If deg(f) is odd, then f has a root in ℝ. Consequently, the only odd degree extension of ℝ is ℝ itself.
proof_sketch: For part 1, f(0) < 0 and f(u) > 0 for large u; by the Intermediate Value Theorem there is c ∈ [0,u] with f(c) = 0. For part 2, assuming positive leading coefficient, limits as x→∞ and x→-∞ go to ∞ and -∞; the Intermediate Value Theorem gives a real root. If L/ℝ is an odd degree extension, take a ∈ L∖ℝ; then [ℝ(a):ℝ] is odd, so the minimal polynomial of a has odd degree and a real root, forcing it to be linear, so a ∈ ℝ and L = ℝ.

name: Properties of closed sets
statement: In a topological space X, the closed sets satisfy: (1) X and ∅ are closed; (2) if A and B are closed, then A ∪ B is closed; (3) if {A_i} is a collection of closed sets, then ⋂_i A_i is closed.
proof_sketch: Follows immediately from the definition of a topology and DeMorgan's laws: complement of union is intersection of complements, and complement of intersection is union of complements.

name: Standard topology on ℝ
statement: A nonempty subset U ⊆ ℝ is open if for every x ∈ U there exists δ > 0 such that (x - δ, x + δ) ⊆ U. Together with ∅, this defines the standard topology on ℝ.
proof_sketch: Easy exercise to verify the topology axioms.

name: Metric space topology
statement: Let (X, d) be a metric space. A nonempty subset U ⊆ X is open if for every x ∈ U there exists δ > 0 such that the open ball B(x, δ) = {y ∈ X : d(x, y) < δ} is contained in U. This defines the metric space topology.
proof_sketch: Straightforward verification using metric space axioms.

name: Finite complement topology
statement: On a set X, the finite complement topology is defined by declaring a proper subset of X closed if it is finite. Equivalently, a nonempty subset is open if its complement is finite.
proof_sketch: Verification: finite unions and arbitrary intersections of finite sets are finite, so closed sets satisfy axioms.

name: Finite-Dimensional Vector Space
statement: If V has a finite basis, then V is said to be a finite dimensional vector space.
proof_sketch: Definition.

name: Theorem 19.14: Existence and Extension of Transcendence Bases
name_norm: existence and extension of transcendence bases
statement: Let K be a field extension of F. (1) There exists a transcendence basis for K/F. (2) If T ⊆ K such that K/F(T) is algebraic, then T contains a transcendence basis for K/F. (3) If S ⊆ K is algebraically independent over F, then S is contained in a transcendence basis of K/F. (4) If S ⊆ T ⊆ K, with S algebraically independent over F and K/F(T) algebraic, then there is a transcendence basis X for K/F with S ⊆ X ⊆ T.
proof_sketch: Statement (4) implies the others by suitable choices of S and T. To prove (4), consider the set of all algebraically independent subsets of T containing S, ordered by inclusion. By Zorn’s lemma it has a maximal element M. If K were not algebraic over F(M), then F(T) would not be algebraic over F(M), so some t∈T is transcendental over F(M), and by Lemma 19.13, M∪{t} is algebraically independent, contradicting maximality. Hence K is algebraic over F(M), making M a transcendence basis within the desired bounds.

name: Chain-complete subset collections have maximal elements
name_norm: chain-complete subset collections have maximal elements
statement: Let X be a set and let S be a nonempty collection of subsets of X, partially ordered by inclusion. If for every chain T in S the union ∪T is an element of S, then S has a maximal element.
proof_sketch: For any chain T, ∪T is an upper bound for T (since each t ∈ T satisfies t ⊆ ∪T) and ∪T ∈ S by hypothesis. Zorn's Lemma then implies S has a maximal element.

name: Chain-complete partial function collections have maximal elements
name_norm: chain-complete partial function collections have maximal elements
statement: Let X,Y be sets and let S be a nonempty collection of pairs (A,f) where A ⊆ X and f: A → Y, ordered by (A,f) ≤ (B,g) iff A ⊆ B and g|A = f. If for every chain T in S the pair (∪T, h) with h defined by h(x) = g(x) whenever (X,g) ∈ T and x ∈ X is an element of S, then S has a maximal element.
proof_sketch: For a chain T, (M, h) with M = ∪T is an upper bound in S by construction (the function h is well-defined because T is a chain). By hypothesis (M,h) ∈ S, so Zorn's Lemma applies.

name: Independence of fiber restriction map for locally compact spaces
name_norm: independence of fiber restriction map for locally compact spaces
statement: Let X be a locally compact Hausdorff space and T a compact connected Hausdorff space. Then the induced homomorphism i_t^* : H_c^*(X × T; F × T) → H_c^*(X; F) is independent of the choice of t ∈ T.
proof_sketch: For any cohomology class a, define K(a) = {t ∈ T : a ∈ Ker i_t^*}. Using 10.6, K(a) is open. Given t and β = i_t^*(a), the class a − π^*β lies in Ker i_t^*, so t ∈ K(a − π^*β). Consequently, for s near t, i_s^*(a) = i_s^*(π^*β) = β, showing s ↦ i_s^*(a) is locally constant. Since T is connected, the value is constant.

name: Leray Sheaves for Vector Bundles
statement: Let ξ be an n-plane bundle over a space Y with total space U. Identify Y with the zero section, set U₀ = U - Y, and let π: U → Y, π₀ = π|U₀. Then the Leray sheaf J^q(π; L) is the constant sheaf with stalks L for q = 0 and 0 otherwise; J^q(π, π₀; L) is the locally constant sheaf with stalks H^q(ℝ^n, ℝ^n - {0}; L).
proof_sketch: Using an admissible local trivialisation V × ℝ^n, homotopy invariance reduces the computation to the disk relative to its boundary. Then 4.2 and 7.1 imply the constant and locally constant nature of the sheaves, with the claimed stalks.

name: Transfer isomorphism for sheaves of L-modules (prime characteristic)
name_norm: transfer isomorphism for sheaves of l-modules (prime characteristic
statement: Let ℱ be a sheaf of L-modules, where L is a field whose characteristic is relatively prime to the order of G. Then the map π*: H_Φ^*(X/G; ℱ) → H_Φ^*(X; π*ℱ)^G is an isomorphism.
proof_sketch: From the relations μ* π*(b) = (ord G) b and π* μ*(a) = Σ g*(a), the image of π* lies in the G-invariants. Since ord G is invertible in L, multiplication by ord G is an isomorphism, hence π* is injective and its image is exactly the invariant subgroup, yielding the isomorphism.

name: Finite group action preserves cohomological local connectedness over a field of prime characteristic
name_norm: finite group action preserves cohomological local connectedness over a field of prime characteristic
statement: If X is a Hausdorff space and n-clc_L (cohomologically locally connected of degree n over a field L) with char(L) relatively prime to ord G, then the orbit space X/G is also n-clc_L.
proof_sketch: For n=0 the statement is clear. For n>0, use the transfer to relate cohomology of neighborhoods in X and X/G. Given a point in X/G and an open neighborhood, lift to X; the G-action and invertibility of |G| in L allow one to average cocycles, showing that the relevant cohomological conditions descend from X to X/G (details in the surrounding proof sketch).

name: Stable Quotient of an Ideal
statement: The stable quotient of I with respect to f, denoted I : f^∞, is the ideal {g ∈ R : there exists n ≥ 1 for which f^n g ∈ I}.
proof_sketch: None (definition).

name: Nakayama's Lemma Corollary (Submodule Equality)
statement: Let Q be a local ring with maximal ideal m, let M be a finitely generated Q-module, and let N be a submodule of M. If M = mM + N, then M = N.
proof_sketch: Observe that m(M/N) = (mM + N)/N. The condition M = mM + N gives m(M/N) = M/N. Applying Nakayama's Lemma to M/N yields M/N = 0, hence M = N.

name: Adapted Monomial Order
statement: A monomial order on k[z1,...,zm,w1,...,wn] is said to be adapted to a given linear function ℓ (presumably tailored for integer programming problems). (The full definition is truncated in the excerpt.)

name: Local convergence of fixed point iteration
name_norm: local convergence of fixed point iteration
statement: If g is differentiable at a fixed point z (g(z) = z) and |g'(z)| < 1, then the iteration z_{k+1} = g(z_k) converges to z for all starting values z_0 sufficiently close to z. Moreover, the smaller |g'(z)| is, the faster the convergence; the case g'(z) = 0 is especially favorable.
proof_sketch: A special case of the Contraction Mapping Theorem; the proof shows that near z the map g is a contraction with constant |g'(z)|+ε < 1.

name: Gröbner Basis Algorithm for Hilbert Bases
name_norm: gröbner basis algorithm for hilbert bases
statement: Let G be a Gröbner basis for I with respect to any elimination order > for which all z_i, t > v_j, and all v_j > w_k. Let S be the subset of G consisting of binomials v^\alpha - w^\alpha for \alpha \in Z_{\ge 0}^N. Then H = \{ \alpha : v^\alpha - w^\alpha \in S \} is the Hilbert basis for K.
proof_sketch: Idea similar to Theorem (1.11); see [Stu1] for full exposition.

name: Pure n-dimensional Polyhedral Complex
statement: A polyhedral complex ∆ ⊂ R^n is said to be pure n-dimensional if every maximal element of ∆ (with respect to inclusion) is an n-dimensional polyhedron.

name: Adjacent Polytopes in a Complex
statement: Two n-dimensional polytopes in a complex ∆ are said to be adjacent (definition continues in text).

name: Graded Resolution
statement: A graded resolution of a graded R-module M is a resolution ... → F_2 → F_1 → F_0 → M → 0 where each F_i is a graded free module and each differential is a graded homomorphism of degree zero.

name: Supporting Hyperplane of a Polytope
statement: A supporting hyperplane H of a convex polytope P ⊂ ℝⁿ is a hyperplane such that P is entirely contained in one of the closed half-spaces determined by H and H ∩ P is non-empty.
proof_sketch: This is a definition; no proof is required.

name: Radical and Radical Ideal
statement: Let I ⊂ k[x1, ..., xn] be an ideal. The radical of I is √I = {g ∈ k[x1, ..., xn] : g^m ∈ I for some integer m ≥ 1}. I is a radical ideal if √I = I.
proof_sketch: N/A

name: Radical Ideal Property
statement: For every ideal I ⊂ k[x1, ..., xn], √I is an ideal containing I.
proof_sketch: See [CLO], Chapter 4, §2.

name: Minimal Element of a Submodule
statement: Let M be a nonzero submodule of k[x]^2. A minimal element of M with respect to a monomial order > is an element g ∈ M \ {0} such that LT(g) is minimal with respect to >.
proof_sketch: None (definition).

name: Order Domain
statement: A pair (R, ρ) is an order domain if ρ is an order function on R.

name: Identity Value of Order Function
statement: ρ(1) = 0, and ρ(f) = 0 if and only if f is a nonzero constant in Fq.
proof_sketch: By the product axiom ρ(1) = ρ(1·1) = ρ(1)+ρ(1) forces ρ(1)=0. If ρ(f)=0, write f in a basis with distinct ρ-values; using linear independence and the bound on ρ of sums forces f to be constant.

name: Linear Independence of Distinct ρ Values
name_norm: linear independence of distinct ρ values
statement: Any set of elements of R with distinct ρ values is linearly independent over Fq.
proof_sketch: Assume a nontrivial linear combination sums to zero. Take the term with maximal ρ-value; by the properties ρ(c·f)=ρ(f) for c≠0 and ρ(f+g) ≤ max(ρ(f),ρ(g)), the maximal ρ-value cannot cancel, contradiction.

name: Definition of a Lattice Polytope
statement: A lattice polytope is a set of the form Conv(A), where A ⊂ ℤⁿ is finite. These are convex hulls of sets of points with integer coordinates.

name: Definition of an Affine Hyperplane
statement: An affine hyperplane in ℝⁿ is defined by an equation of the form m·ν = −a, where ν is a nonzero vector in ℝⁿ and a is a real number.

name: Definition of Supporting Hyperplane and Inward Normal
name_norm: definition of supporting hyperplane and inward normal
statement: For a polytope Q ⊂ ℝⁿ and a nonzero vector ν ∈ ℝⁿ, let a_Q(ν) = −min_{m∈Q} (m·ν). The equation m·ν = −a_Q(ν) is called a supporting hyperplane of Q, and ν is called an inward pointing normal.

name: Uniqueness of Products up to Isomorphism
name_norm: uniqueness of products up to isomorphism
statement: If A and B are both products of the same family {A_i}, then A and B are isomorphic.
proof_sketch: By the universal property of A, there is a unique θ: B → A making the product diagrams commute. Symmetrically, there is a unique η: A → B. Then θ η and η θ are morphisms into the products themselves; by uniqueness of the mediating morphism for the identity maps, θ η = id_A and η θ = id_B, so they are isomorphisms.
depends_on: product of a family of objects

name: Ker-Coker Exact Sequence
statement: Given a commutative diagram with exact columns, set K_i = ker φ_i and L_i = coker φ_i. Then the induced sequence K_1 → K_2 → K_3 → L_1 → L_2 → L_3 is exact.
proof_sketch: Form a double complex with the given diagram and zero elsewhere; rows are complexes and columns are short exact. The homology at B_j is K_j and at C_j is L_j. Applying the long exact homology sequence (Theorem 3.3) yields the exact sequence connecting them.

name: Construction of local coordinate charts on Y(Γ)
name_norm: construction of local coordinate charts on y(γ
statement: For a congruence subgroup Γ of SL2(Z) and any point π(τ) ∈ Y(Γ), let U be a neighborhood as in Corollary 2.2.3, set h = |Γτ|, and define δ(z) = (z - τ)/(z - τ̅) (a map taking τ to 0 and τ̅ to ∞). Then ψ(z) = (δ(z))^h identifies points of U exactly under the Γ-action, inducing a homeomorphism ϕ: π(U) → ψ(U) ⊂ C that serves as a local coordinate chart on the modular curve Y(Γ).
proof_sketch: By Proposition 2.2.2 the stabilizer Γτ is cyclic of order h, and δ conjugates it to rotations z ↦ e^{2πi d/h} z. Corollary 2.2.3 guarantees that any identification in U comes from Γτ. Hence π(z1) = π(z2) ⇔ δ(z1) = e^{2πi d/h} δ(z2) ⇔ ψ(z1) = ψ(z2). The map ψ is open by the Open Mapping Theorem, yielding a bijective continuous open map ϕ defined by ϕ(π(z)) = ψ(z), which is therefore a homeomorphism.

name: Connectedness of X(Γ)
statement: The extended upper half-plane H* is connected, and therefore its continuous image X(Γ) is connected.
proof_sketch: Assume H* = O1 ∪ O2 with disjoint open sets. Intersecting with connected H forces one to contain H, so the other is contained in Q ∪ {∞}. But any nonempty subset of Q ∪ {∞} cannot be open in H*; thus one is empty, proving H* connected. X(Γ) is a continuous image, hence connected.

name: Dirichlet L-function
statement: For a Dirichlet character χ modulo N and Re(s) > 1, L(s, χ) = ∑_{n=1}^∞ χ(n) n^{-s} = ∏_{p prime} (1 - χ(p) p^{-s})^{-1}.

name: Hilbert Basis Theorem (Special Case for Polynomial Rings)
name_norm: hilbert basis theorem (special case for polynomial rings
statement: The ring k[x1, ..., xn] is Noetherian, meaning that all ideals are finitely generated, or equivalently there is no infinite strictly increasing chain I1 ⊂ I2 ⊂ I3 ⊂ · · · of ideals.
proof_sketch: Standard proof, e.g., by induction on n; see Fulton [Ful69].

name: Relation of Orders of Vanishing under Norm
name_norm: relation of orders of vanishing under norm
statement: For any nonzero f ∈ C(X)^*, ν_y(norm_h f) = ∑_{x ∈ h^{-1}(y)} ν_x(f).
proof_sketch: Exercise 6.2.2(b).

name: Cosets Partition a Group
statement: Let H be a subgroup of a group G. The left cosets of H form a partition of G; the right cosets of H form a partition of G.
proof_sketch: Define relation xRy iff xy^{-1} ∈ H. R is reflexive (xx^{-1}=1∈H), symmetric (xy^{-1}∈H ⇒ yx^{-1}=(xy^{-1})^{-1}∈H), transitive (xy^{-1}, yz^{-1}∈H ⇒ xz^{-1}=(xy^{-1})(yz^{-1})∈H). Thus R is an equivalence relation. xRy iff x ∈ Hy, so equivalence classes are right cosets. Left cosets are obtained similarly from xLy iff y^{-1}x ∈ H.

name: Uniqueness of the Identity Element
statement: A binary operation has at most one identity element; if an identity exists, it is unique.
proof_sketch: Suppose e and f are both identity elements. Then e = e·f = f, because e is an identity gives e·f = f, and f is an identity gives e·f = e.

name: Inverse of Inverse and Product Inverse
name_norm: inverse of inverse and product inverse
proof_sketch: From uv = 1 follows v = u^{−1}. Thus x^{−1}x = 1 gives x = (x^{−1})^{−1}. For n=2, (xy)(y^{−1}x^{−1}) = 1, so y^{−1}x^{−1} = (xy)^{−1}; general case by induction.

name: Powers with Integer Exponents
statement: For a ∈ G: if n ≥ 0, a^n is the product of n copies of a (a^0 = 1, a^1 = a); if n < 0 with n = −m (m ≥ 0), then a^n = (a^m)^{−1}. In particular, a^{−1} is the inverse.
proof_sketch: None.

name: Consequences of Injectivity and Surjectivity for Homomorphisms
name_norm: consequences of injectivity and surjectivity for homomorphisms
